mnemonics.title: Vfork Memory Sharing
mnemonics.content: V - Virtual (no real copy), F - Fork without copying, O - Overlapped (shared) memory, R - Restricted (child must exec)

mnemonics.title: Return Value Pattern
mnemonics.content: PARENT gets CHILD's PID (Positive number), CHILD gets ZERO - P-CHILD-ZERO helps remember: 'Parent Calls Help: Zero'

acronyms.expansion: Virtual FORK - creates process without copying virtual memory
acronyms.usage: Used when child immediately calls exec() to replace its memory space

acronyms.expansion: Copy-On-Write - technique used by fork() to delay memory copying
acronyms.usage: Modern fork() uses COW to achieve performance similar to vfork()

acronyms.expansion: Process Identifier - unique number identifying a process
acronyms.usage: vfork() returns child's PID to parent, 0 to child

visualTips.visualization: Imagine two people standing in the SAME room (address space) vs fork() where each has their OWN room. vfork: Parent's room is borrowed, fork: Two separate rooms created.
Draw timeline: vfork called → Parent suspended (paused) → Child runs → Child calls exec or exits → Parent resumes. This shows blocking behavior.
Like a two-way radio: Parent hears 'CHILD'S ID' (positive), Child hears '0' (zero) - one call, two different responses.

analogies.analogy: vfork() is like photocopying a document by just letting someone use the original (risky but fast), while fork() is like making an actual photocopy (slower but safer).
vfork() is like a parent lending their body to the child temporarily - the parent must wait because they're sharing everything.
exec() after vfork() is like moving into a new house (new program) after temporarily staying at the parent's house - you finally get your own space.
